section.title: Melchizedek
section.sectionRationale: This chapter is a single connected episode: a war of kings, Lot's capture, Abram's daring rescue, and the meeting with the mysterious priest-king Melchizedek. It belongs together because the whole narrative builds to that encounter, where Abram is blessed by God Most High and refuses the spoils of Sodom.
section.thesis: Abram routs a coalition of kings to rescue Lot, then meets Melchizedek, priest of God Most High, whose blessing and bread and wine lift the story from war to worship and point ahead to a greater priest-king, Jesus.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the war of the nine kings and Lot's capture; Abram's rescue with his household; the blessing of Melchizedek and Abram's tithe; and Abram's refusal of the king of Sodom's reward.

units.label: The war of the kings
units.note: Lot swept up in Sodom's fall

verses.text: In those days Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim
went to war against Bera king of Sodom, Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (that is, Zoar).
The latter five came as allies to the Valley of Siddim (that is, the Salt Sea).
For twelve years they had been subject to Chedorlaomer, but in the thirteenth year they rebelled.
In the fourteenth year, Chedorlaomer and the kings allied with him went out and defeated the Rephaites in Ashteroth-karnaim, the Zuzites in Ham, the Emites in Shaveh-kiriathaim,
and the Horites in the area of Mount Seir, as far as El-paran, which is near the desert.
Then they turned back to invade En-mishpat (that is, Kadesh), and they conquered the whole territory of the Amalekites, as well as the Amorites who lived in Hazazon-tamar.
Then the king of Sodom, the king of Gomorrah, the king of Admah, the king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (that is, Zoar) marched out and arrayed themselves for battle in the Valley of Siddim
against Chedorlaomer king of Elam, Tidal king of Goiim, Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar, four kings against five.
Now the Valley of Siddim was full of tar pits, and as the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, some men fell into the pits, but the survivors fled to the hill country.
The four kings seized all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah and all their food, and they went on their way.
They also carried off Abram’s nephew Lot and his possessions, since Lot was living in Sodom.
Then an escapee came and reported this to Abram the Hebrew. Now Abram was living near the Oaks of Mamre the Amorite, a brother of Eshcol and Aner, all of whom were bound by treaty to Abram.
And when Abram heard that his relative had been captured, he mobilized the 318 trained men born in his household, and they set out in pursuit as far as Dan.
During the night, Abram divided his forces and routed Chedorlaomer’s army, pursuing them as far as Hobah, north of Damascus.
He retrieved all the goods, as well as his relative Lot and his possessions, together with the women and the rest of the people.
After Abram returned from defeating Chedorlaomer and the kings allied with him, the king of Sodom went out to meet him in the Valley of Shaveh (that is, the King’s Valley).
Then {{melchizedek|Melchizedek}} king of Salem brought out bread and wine, since he was {{kohen|priest}} of {{el-elyon|God Most High}},
and he blessed Abram and said: “Blessed be Abram by God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth,
and blessed be God Most High, who has delivered your enemies into your hand.” Then Abram gave Melchizedek {{maaser|a tenth}} of everything.

units.label: Abram and the king of Sodom

verses.text: The king of Sodom said to Abram, “Give me the people, but take the goods for yourself.”
But Abram replied to the king of Sodom, “I have raised my hand to the LORD God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth,
that I will not accept even a thread, or a strap of a sandal, or anything that belongs to you, lest you should say, ‘I have made Abram rich.’
I will accept nothing but what my men have eaten and the share for the men who went with me, Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre. They may take their portion.”

melchizedek.gloss: The name means king of righteousness, and as king of Salem he is also king of peace. He appears without recorded genealogy, both priest and king, which is why Psalm 110 and Hebrews 7 take him as a type of Christ, a priest forever in the order of Melchizedek.

kohen.gloss: A priest, one who mediates between God and people. This is the first time the word appears in the Bible, and strikingly it names not an Israelite but a Canaanite king who worships the true God, a priesthood older than Levi that foreshadows the everlasting priesthood of Jesus.

el-elyon.gloss: God Most High, the sovereign Creator of heaven and earth. Melchizedek worships Him, and Abram identifies this Most High God with the LORD, Yahweh, in verse 22, confessing that the God of the covenant is the one supreme God over all kings and nations.

maaser.gloss: A tenth, a tithe. Abram gives a tenth of the spoils to Melchizedek, an act Hebrews 7 uses to argue that Melchizedek's priesthood, and so Christ's, is greater than Levi's, since Levi in a sense tithed through Abram to Melchizedek.

basic.context: Four powerful kings sweep through the region and defeat the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah, carrying off loot and captives, including Abram's nephew Lot. Abram hears of it, gathers 318 men from his household, and rescues Lot in a daring night raid. On his way home he meets a mysterious priest-king named Melchizedek.
basic.hermeneutics: The key question is who Melchizedek is and why he matters so much. He is king of Salem and priest of God Most High, and he blesses Abram with bread and wine. The rest of the Bible treats him as a picture of Christ, a priest and king who serves the true God.
basic.originalLanguages: Melchizedek means king of righteousness, and Salem means peace, so he is king of righteousness and peace. He is the first priest, kohen, named in the Bible, and he serves God Most High, El Elyon, the Creator of heaven and earth. Abram gives him a tithe, a tenth of everything.
basic.typology: Melchizedek is one of the clearest pictures of Jesus in the Old Testament. He is both king and priest, he brings out bread and wine, and he has no recorded beginning or end. Psalm 110 and Hebrews say Jesus is a priest forever in the order of Melchizedek, greater than the priests of Israel.
basic.christ: Jesus is the true King of righteousness and Prince of peace, and He is our great High Priest, not from the line of Levi but in the order of Melchizedek. The bread and wine Melchizedek brings out point ahead to the Lord's Supper. In Jesus, kingship and priesthood come together forever.
basic.god: God is God Most High, the Creator of heaven and earth, sovereign over every king and army in the story. He gives Abram the victory, and He is worshipped by Melchizedek. He is the God who provides, so that Abram can refuse Sodom's riches and trust Him alone.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 110 calls the coming King a priest forever in the order of Melchizedek. Hebrews 7 unfolds this at length. And the bread and wine anticipate the meal Jesus gives His people.
basic.oneStory: In a chapter of war and plunder, a priest-king suddenly appears, blesses Abram, and lifts the story to worship. Melchizedek is a signpost to Jesus, the King and Priest who blesses His people and gives them bread and wine, and Abram's refusal of Sodom shows the faith that trusts God Most High rather than the world's rewards.

context.literary: The chapter shifts from the personal, family scale of Abram's story to the sweeping stage of international war, then narrows again to a single, weighty encounter. Two kings meet Abram: the king of Sodom, who offers goods, and Melchizedek, who offers blessing; Abram's response to each reveals his faith.
context.historical: The named kings and campaign fit the world of the ancient Near East, where eastern overlords raided the fertile trade routes of Canaan. Salem is generally identified with Jerusalem, and God Most High, El Elyon, was a title for the supreme God that Abram unites with the covenant name, the LORD.
context.canonical: Melchizedek appears again only in Psalm 110 and Hebrews 5 to 7, where he becomes the key to understanding Christ's priesthood as older, higher, and more permanent than the Levitical order. This brief scene thus carries enormous theological weight across the canon.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Abram as a man of courageous faith who rescues his kinsman, worships God Most High through Melchizedek, and refuses to be enriched by Sodom, and to introduce the priest-king who will become a central type of Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Abram's rescue, his tithe, and his refusal. His worship and trust are commended as exemplary, while the military details are descriptive of the events, not a template for conduct.

debates.question: Who is Melchizedek?

views.label: A historical Canaanite priest-king, a type of Christ
views.case: Most read him as a real king of Salem who worshipped the true God, presented without genealogy so that he foreshadows Christ's eternal priesthood, as Hebrews 7 argues from the silence of the text.

views.label: A preincarnate appearance of the Son
views.case: Some suggest he was Christ Himself appearing before the incarnation, since Hebrews says he was made like the Son of God and remains a priest forever.

debates.bearing: Hebrews stresses that he was made like the Son of God, which favours seeing him as a real man who is a type, not the Son in person; either way the point is that his priesthood pictures and prepares for Christ's.

study.originalLanguages: The encounter is dense with meaning. Melchizedek, malki-tsedeq, means king of righteousness, and as king of Salem, shalom, he is king of peace. He is the first kohen, priest, named in Scripture, and he serves El Elyon, God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth, whom Abram then openly identifies with the LORD, the covenant God. Abram's gift of a maaser, a tenth, becomes in Hebrews the proof that this priesthood outranks Levi's. So the words themselves gather kingship, priesthood, righteousness, and peace into one figure who can only be fully answered by Christ.

typology.type: Melchizedek, king of righteousness and king of Salem
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true King of righteousness and Prince of peace, Hebrews 7:2, Isaiah 9:6.

typology.type: A priest without recorded beginning or end
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, a priest forever in the order of Melchizedek, Psalm 110:4, Hebrews 7:3.

typology.type: The bread and wine Melchizedek brings out
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the bread and cup of the Lord's Supper, which Christ our priest-king gives to His people.

study.christ: This chapter gives one of the richest previews of Jesus in Genesis. Melchizedek is both king and priest, offices held apart in Israel but united in Christ, who is King of righteousness and Prince of peace and also our great High Priest. Because Melchizedek appears with no recorded lineage, Psalm 110 and Hebrews present his order as eternal, and they declare Jesus a priest forever in that order, superior to the temporary Levitical priesthood. His bread and wine foreshadow the Lord's Supper, and his blessing of Abram shows the greater blessing Christ speaks over all who are Abram's children by faith.
study.god: God is revealed as El Elyon, God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth, and therefore sovereign over the whole clash of empires in the chapter. He hands Abram's enemies into his hand, and He is worshipped by a Gentile priest, showing that the true God is not tribal but supreme over all peoples. Because this God is Abram's provider, Abram can raise his hand in oath and refuse Sodom's wealth, resting his security in the Most High rather than in the gifts of a doomed king.

crossReferences.note: The LORD swears the coming King is a priest forever in the order of Melchizedek.
Melchizedek's blessing and Abram's tithe prove this priesthood, and Christ's, greater than Levi's.
Jesus holds a permanent priesthood and always lives to intercede, unlike the many mortal Levitical priests.
The promised child is Prince of Peace, ruling in righteousness, the reality Melchizedek pictures.

study.oneStory: Amid a chapter of war and plunder, a priest-king of Salem steps forward, blesses Abram, and turns the scene into worship of God Most High. Melchizedek is a bright signpost pointing to Jesus, the King and Priest forever who blesses His people, gives them bread and wine, and secures their peace with God. And Abram's refusal of Sodom's spoils shows the faith the whole Bible commends, trusting the Creator of heaven and earth rather than the rewards of a perishing world.

quiz.q: Why did Abram set out with 318 men?

options.text: To rescue his nephew Lot, who had been captured by the four kings.
options.note: Abram acted to save his kinsman swept up in Sodom's defeat.

options.text: To conquer Sodom and take its throne for himself.
options.note: Abram sought to rescue Lot, not to seize a kingdom.

options.text: To plunder the retreating kings for their wealth.
options.note: He later refuses the spoils; his aim was rescue, not plunder.

options.text: To defend his household and allies from attack.
options.note: There is a protective element, but the clear purpose was to rescue Lot.

quiz.q: Who is Melchizedek in this chapter?

options.text: King of Salem and priest of God Most High who blesses Abram.
options.note: He is both king and priest and serves the true God.

options.text: The king of Sodom offering Abram a reward.
options.note: That is a different king; Melchizedek offers blessing, not spoils.

options.text: One of the four invading kings from the east.
options.note: He is not among the warring kings; he is the priest-king of Salem.

options.text: A mysterious priest-king the Bible later links to Christ.
options.note: True and important, though the passage first identifies him as king of Salem and priest of God Most High.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament use Melchizedek?

options.text: As a type of Christ, a priest forever whose order is greater than Levi's.
options.note: Psalm 110 and Hebrews 7 present Jesus as a priest in Melchizedek's order.

options.text: As an example of a bad king to avoid.
options.note: He is honoured, not condemned; he blesses Abram and points to Christ.

options.text: As the founder of the Levitical priesthood.
options.note: His priesthood is older than and distinct from Levi's.

options.text: As a picture of Jesus who unites king and priest.
options.note: True and central, though the fuller point is the superiority of his priestly order.

quiz.q: Why did Abram refuse the king of Sodom's goods?

options.text: So no one could say Sodom, not God Most High, had made him rich.
options.note: Abram trusted God as his provider rather than a doomed king's reward.

options.text: Because the goods were too heavy to carry home.
options.note: His reason was faith and reputation, not the weight of the loot.

options.text: Because he had already taken enough plunder secretly.
options.note: Abram took nothing for himself; the refusal was principled.

options.text: To keep his trust and security in the LORD alone.
options.note: True and close to the heart, expressed in his concern that God, not Sodom, be his enricher.

quiz.q: What do the bread and wine of Melchizedek point toward?

options.text: The Lord's Supper that Christ, our priest-king, gives His people.
options.note: The bread and wine foreshadow the meal Jesus institutes.

options.text: A bribe to win Abram's loyalty.
options.note: They are part of a priestly blessing, not a bribe.

options.text: Ordinary provisions with no deeper meaning.
options.note: In context and in Hebrews they carry rich significance.

options.text: A sign of fellowship and blessing from God Most High.
options.note: True at one level, and ultimately they anticipate Christ's supper.

questions: Abram risked everything to rescue Lot, the relative who had chosen the best land for himself. Who is God calling you to help, even someone who once wronged or overlooked you?
After the battle, Melchizedek lifted Abram's eyes from war to worship. What practices help you return to worship after stressful or draining seasons?
Abram refused Sodom's wealth so his security would rest in God alone. Where are you tempted to let the world, rather than God, be the source of your confidence?
Jesus is our King and Priest forever, always alive to bless and intercede for us. How does it steady you to know you have a priest-king praying for you right now?
Melchizedek's bread and wine point to the Lord's Supper. How might coming to that table with fresh eyes deepen your trust in Christ this week?

translationNotes.note: God Most High renders the Hebrew El Elyon; Abram in verse 22 joins this title to the covenant name, the LORD, confessing that Melchizedek's Most High God is the same God who called him.

A tenth of everything is the first tithe in Scripture; Hebrews 7 builds on it to argue that Melchizedek's priesthood, and therefore Christ's, is greater than the Levitical priesthood descended from Abram.
